name: Norris Lake
description:
Norris Lake is a 34,200-acre crown jewel of the Tennessee Valley Authority system and the first reservoir ever built by the TVA, impounded in 1936 on the Clinch and Powell Rivers across five counties in the rugged Cumberland Plateau and Appalachian foothills. With an extraordinary 809 miles of shoreline and depths plunging to 200 feet, Norris Lake is renowned for its crystal-clear mountain water that consistently ranks among the cleanest large reservoirs in the southeastern United States. The towns of Rocky Top, Jacksboro, La Follette, and Norris provide a full range of services, marinas, and lakeside accommodations for visiting anglers.

The bass fishing at Norris Lake is exceptional across multiple species, with Largemouth, Smallmouth, and Spotted Bass all thriving in the lake's diverse habitat of rocky bluffs, submerged timber, and deep channel ledges. Striped Bass grow to impressive sizes feeding on the abundant threadfin shad population in the main lake's open water, with fish exceeding 30 pounds caught each season. Walleye fishing is outstanding in the deeper channel areas, particularly during the cooler months, while Crappie and Bluegill provide reliable panfish action in the numerous coves and marina areas throughout the year.

Norris Lake offers dozens of public boat ramps and full-service marinas spread across its five-county footprint, ensuring anglers can quickly access virtually any section of the lake. Norris Dam State Park provides excellent launch facilities and camping at the dam end of the lake. Whether you are working a jerkbait along a rocky bluff for Smallmouth Bass, trolling umbrella rigs for Striped Bass in the main channel, or filling a livewell with slab Crappie in a sheltered cove, Norris Lake delivers world-class fishing in the clear mountain waters of East Tennessee.

name: Cherokee Lake
description:
Cherokee Lake is a 28,300-acre TVA reservoir on the Holston River in the rolling hills of upper East Tennessee, spanning four counties with 400 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 160 feet near the dam. This productive warm-water fishery sits in the agricultural heartland between the Great Smoky Mountains and the Cumberland Plateau, offering a diverse mix of bass, walleye, and panfish species in a setting of pastoral beauty. The cities of Morristown, Jefferson City, and Rogersville serve as convenient base camps for anglers exploring this expansive lake.

Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass provide excellent fishing throughout Cherokee Lake, with Largemouths holding in the numerous creek arms and shallow coves while Smallmouths patrol the main lake's rocky points and bluffs. Walleye and Sauger are highly prized by winter and early spring anglers who target these species in the deeper channel areas and tailwater below Cherokee Dam. Crappie fishing is outstanding during the spring spawn when fish push into shallow brush and stakebeds in the upper portions of the creek arms. Catfish and Striped Bass round out the fishery, with flathead and channel cats reaching impressive sizes in the deeper river channel.

Public boat ramps are available at numerous locations around Cherokee Lake, with popular access points at Point Park, Poor Valley, and several county and state-managed facilities. Full-service marinas provide fuel, tackle, and boat rentals. Whether you are casting topwater baits for Largemouth Bass in a misty morning creek arm, vertical jigging for Walleye over deep channel ledges, or setting jugs for Catfish on a summer night, Cherokee Lake offers reliable year-round fishing in the scenic hills of East Tennessee.

name: Douglas Lake
description:
Douglas Lake is a 28,420-acre TVA reservoir on the French Broad River in the foothills of the Great Smoky Mountains, offering 513 miles of shoreline across three East Tennessee counties and serving as the gateway between the productive farmland of the Tennessee Valley and the soaring peaks of the Smokies. With depths reaching 150 feet near the dam, this highly fertile lake experiences significant seasonal drawdowns that expose vast mud flats in winter but concentrate fish into predictable patterns that knowledgeable anglers exploit with great success. The historic town of Dandridge, Tennessee's second-oldest town, sits on the lake's shore alongside the resort community of Sevierville.

Largemouth Bass fishing at Douglas Lake is among the best in East Tennessee, with the lake's fertile waters and abundant cover producing excellent numbers of quality fish, particularly during the spring spawn when bass move shallow in the creek arms. Crappie fishing is equally outstanding, with massive schools of black and white Crappie stacking up around brush piles, stakebeds, and bridge pilings during the spring migration. Catfish reach trophy proportions in the deeper channel areas, while Sauger and Walleye provide excellent cool-weather fishing in the river channel and around the dam.

Boat launches are plentiful around Douglas Lake, with state and county ramps at Dandridge, Rankin, and several other locations providing reliable access even during winter drawdown periods. The lake's proximity to Sevierville and the Great Smoky Mountains National Park makes it a popular destination for families combining fishing with mountain vacations. Whether you are flipping jigs for Largemouth Bass in flooded timber, pulling Crappie from submerged brush piles, or anchoring in the channel for heavyweight Catfish, Douglas Lake delivers productive fishing just minutes from the Smokies.

name: Fort Loudoun Lake
description:
Fort Loudoun Lake is Knoxville's home lake, a 14,600-acre TVA reservoir on the Tennessee River that flows directly through the heart of Tennessee's third-largest city across three counties. With 360 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 75 feet, this urban fishery provides the unique experience of catching quality bass, Crappie, and Catfish within sight of the University of Tennessee campus and downtown Knoxville's skyline. The lake stretches from Fort Loudoun Dam near Lenoir City upstream through Knoxville, offering both urban waterfront fishing and quieter rural stretches in Loudon and Blount counties.

Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass fishing on Fort Loudoun Lake benefits from the lake's extensive dock systems, riprap banks, and rocky shoreline structure that provide year-round holding areas for both species. Crappie fishing is particularly popular during the spring spawn when fish flood into the many residential docks and marina areas. Sauger provide excellent winter fishing in the deeper channel areas near the dam, and Catfish grow to impressive sizes throughout the lake. Striped Bass are an exciting bonus species, with fish chasing surface-feeding schools of shad during fall months.

Fort Loudoun Lake offers numerous public boat ramps operated by TVA, Knox County, and various municipalities, with major facilities at Volunteer Landing in downtown Knoxville, Concord Park, and Louisville Point Park near Lenoir City. Full-service marinas are scattered throughout the lake. Whether you are casting to dock pilings for Largemouth Bass on a Saturday morning, jigging for Sauger near the dam during winter, or enjoying a sunset Crappie bite from the Knoxville waterfront, Fort Loudoun Lake puts quality fishing within arm's reach of East Tennessee's largest metropolitan area.

name: Watts Bar Lake
description:
Watts Bar Lake is a sprawling 39,090-acre TVA reservoir on the Tennessee River in the scenic hill country of middle East Tennessee, stretching across four counties with an impressive 783 miles of shoreline. With depths reaching 70 feet in the main channel, this massive lake encompasses the confluence of the Clinch and Emory Rivers with the Tennessee River, creating a complex network of arms, channels, and embayments that support one of the most diverse warm-water fisheries in the TVA system. The towns of Spring City, Kingston, and Rockwood provide convenient access to different sections of this expansive waterway.

Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass are abundant throughout Watts Bar Lake, with the numerous creek arms and rocky shoreline providing excellent habitat for both species. Sauger fishing is a wintertime tradition on Watts Bar, with anglers vertical jigging in the deep channel areas near the dam and at the mouths of the Clinch and Emory Rivers for these golden-hued predators. Crappie fishing is outstanding during spring, with fish congregating around submerged brush, stakebeds, and dock pilings in the upper reaches of the creek arms. Catfish and Striped Bass add size and variety to the fishery, with trophy-class fish taken from the deeper main-channel waters.

Public boat ramps are well distributed around Watts Bar Lake, with TVA and county-operated facilities at Spring City, Kingston, and numerous locations along the Clinch and Emory River arms. Several full-service marinas provide fuel, supplies, and guide services. Whether you are running the vast main lake for Striped Bass breaking on surface shad, working a worm along the rocky Clinch River arm for Smallmouth Bass, or pulling limits of Sauger from the deep water near the dam, Watts Bar Lake offers big-water fishing opportunities across a broad and beautiful Tennessee landscape.

name: Chickamauga Lake
description:
Chickamauga Lake is a 35,400-acre TVA reservoir on the Tennessee River that has earned a national reputation as one of the premier bass fishing lakes in America, consistently hosting major professional bass tournaments and producing trophy-class Largemouth Bass. With 784 miles of shoreline across four counties and depths reaching 55 feet, this expansive lake stretches from Chickamauga Dam near Chattanooga upstream through the scenic Tennessee River Valley. The Scenic City of Chattanooga anchors the southern end of the lake, while the historic town of Dayton and the community of Soddy-Daisy provide additional lakeside access.

Largemouth Bass are the undisputed star of Chickamauga Lake, with the lake producing a remarkable number of fish over 5 pounds and regularly giving up specimens exceeding 10 pounds during the spring spawn. The lake's fertile, relatively shallow waters and extensive grass beds create ideal conditions for growing trophy bass at a rate that rivals the best fisheries in the country. Smallmouth Bass provide outstanding fishing along the main lake's rocky points, while Crappie, Bluegill, Sauger, Catfish, and Striped Bass ensure that anglers of all preferences find excellent fishing opportunities throughout the year.

Boat ramps are abundant on Chickamauga Lake, with major public facilities at Chester Frost Park near Chattanooga, Sale Creek, and Dayton providing well-maintained launches and ample parking. The lake's proximity to Chattanooga makes it easily accessible via Interstate 75, and numerous marinas and guide services cater to visiting tournament anglers and recreational fishermen alike. Whether you are sight-fishing for spawning Largemouth Bass in the grass flats, deep cranking the main lake ledges in summer, or pulling slabs of Crappie from brush piles during fall, Chickamauga Lake delivers nationally recognized bass fishing in the Tennessee River Valley.

name: Nickajack Lake
description:
Nickajack Lake is a 10,370-acre TVA reservoir on the Tennessee River in Marion County, positioned in the dramatic gorge where the river cuts through the Cumberland Plateau near the tri-state intersection of Tennessee, Alabama, and Georgia. With 192 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 45 feet, this scenic lake is flanked by towering sandstone bluffs and forested mountains that create a stunning backdrop for fishing. The small communities of South Pittsburg, Jasper, and Kimball serve as quiet gateways to this underrated fishery that receives far less pressure than its more famous neighbors upstream.

Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass fishing on Nickajack Lake benefits from relatively light fishing pressure and excellent habitat, with bass holding around rocky bluffs, laydown timber, and the numerous creek arm channels that feed into the main river. Catfish are a major draw, with blue, channel, and flathead cats thriving in the lake's deeper waters and providing outstanding fishing for anglers soaking cut bait and live shad in the main channel. Crappie congregate around submerged structure and bridge pilings, particularly during spring, while Sauger provide consistent cool-weather fishing near the dam.

Public boat ramps are available at Shellmound and several other locations around the lake, with the facilities being well maintained but often less crowded than ramps on larger nearby reservoirs. The lake's position in the Tennessee River gorge provides scenic beauty that rivals any lake in the state. Whether you are working the bluff walls for Smallmouth Bass, anchoring in the channel for trophy Blue Catfish, or quietly slipping through a creek arm for Crappie, Nickajack Lake offers peaceful, productive fishing in one of Tennessee's most picturesque river gorge settings.

name: Dale Hollow Lake
description:
Dale Hollow Lake is a 27,700-acre U.S. Army Corps of Engineers reservoir on the Obey River that holds the distinction of producing the all-time world record Smallmouth Bass, an 11-pound 15-ounce giant caught in 1955 that has never been surpassed. With 620 miles of shoreline across four counties on the Tennessee-Kentucky border and depths reaching 130 feet, this clear mountain lake is nestled in the rugged hills of the Upper Cumberland region and is widely regarded as one of the finest Smallmouth Bass fisheries in the world. The towns of Celina, Livingston, and Byrdstown provide services and accommodations for the dedicated anglers who travel from across the country to fish these legendary waters.

Smallmouth Bass are the main event at Dale Hollow, with the lake's crystal-clear water, rocky structure, and abundant crawfish forage producing bronzebacks that routinely exceed 4 pounds and occasionally push past 7 pounds. Largemouth Bass inhabit the back portions of the creek arms and provide excellent fishing around wood cover and vegetation. Walleye are an exciting target in the deeper main lake areas, particularly during winter and early spring. Musky have been stocked to create a trophy fishery, with these apex predators reaching impressive sizes. Rainbow and Brown Trout thrive in the cold tailwater below the dam, creating a bonus fishery that draws fly anglers.

Dale Hollow Lake offers numerous public boat ramps and full-service marinas, including facilities at Dale Hollow Dam, Willow Grove, and Star Point, spread across both the Tennessee and Kentucky shores. The lake's clear water and scenic mountain setting make it equally popular for swimming and scuba diving. Whether you are drop-shotting a ledge for trophy Smallmouth Bass, trolling deep-diving crankbaits for Walleye, or casting oversized lures for Musky in the crystal-clear depths, Dale Hollow Lake offers world-record-quality fishing in the beautiful Upper Cumberland highlands of Tennessee.

name: Center Hill Lake
description:
Center Hill Lake is an 18,220-acre U.S. Army Corps of Engineers reservoir on the Caney Fork River in the rolling hills of middle Tennessee, spanning five counties with 415 miles of shoreline and depths reaching an impressive 175 feet. This deep, clear-water lake sits in the Highland Rim region east of Nashville and is known for its excellent water quality, dramatic limestone bluffs, and outstanding multi-species fishing that draws anglers year-round. The town of Smithville serves as the primary gateway to the lake, with Silver Point and Lancaster providing additional access to the upper reaches.

Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass both thrive at Center Hill Lake, with Smallmouths favoring the main lake's rocky bluffs and points while Largemouths concentrate in the creek arms and shallow coves. Walleye fishing is exceptional, particularly during late winter and early spring when these fish move shallow to spawn along gravel banks and rocky shorelines. Striped Bass provide thrilling open-water action when they push threadfin shad to the surface during the summer and fall months. Crappie and Catfish round out the diverse fishery, with Crappie fishing particularly productive in the spring around submerged brush and standing timber.

Center Hill Lake offers well-maintained public boat ramps at Edgar Evins State Park, Center Hill Dam, and numerous other locations around the lake. Edgar Evins State Park provides camping, cabins, a marina, and boat rentals for visiting anglers. Whether you are working the limestone bluffs for Smallmouth Bass, trolling for Walleye in the deep main channel, or chasing Striped Bass boils on a summer evening, Center Hill Lake delivers excellent deep-water fishing in the scenic hills of middle Tennessee.

name: Old Hickory Lake
description:
Old Hickory Lake is Nashville's premier fishing lake, a 22,500-acre U.S. Army Corps of Engineers reservoir on the Cumberland River that stretches across five counties through the rapidly growing suburbs east and north of Tennessee's capital city. With 440 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 60 feet, this productive warm-water fishery winds through rolling Middle Tennessee countryside and provides convenient fishing access for the Nashville metropolitan area's two million residents. The communities of Hendersonville, Mt. Juliet, and Gallatin line the lake's shores and offer full services for visiting anglers.

Largemouth Bass fishing on Old Hickory Lake is consistently productive, with the lake's abundant dock systems, riprap shorelines, and creek arm structure providing excellent habitat throughout the seasons. Crappie fishing is a beloved spring tradition, with anglers pulling slabs from brush piles, bridge pilings, and dock structures across the lake. Catfish of all three major species grow to impressive sizes in the Cumberland River channel, and Sauger provide reliable cool-weather action near the dam and in the deeper channel areas. Striped Bass add big-fish excitement, with schools of these hard-fighting predators chasing shad across the main lake during fall.

Old Hickory Lake offers numerous public boat ramps operated by the Corps of Engineers, TWRA, and local municipalities, with major facilities at Rockland Recreation Area, Old Hickory Beach, and Bledsoe Creek State Park in Gallatin. The lake's proximity to downtown Nashville via Interstate 65 and State Route 386 makes it an easy day trip from the city. Whether you are casting around docks for Largemouth Bass in a suburban creek arm, trolling for Striped Bass on the open lake, or spending a quiet morning pulling Crappie from brush piles near Gallatin, Old Hickory Lake puts quality fishing within easy reach of Music City.

name: Percy Priest Lake
description:
Percy Priest Lake is a 14,200-acre U.S. Army Corps of Engineers reservoir on Stones River that sits squarely in the heart of the Nashville metropolitan area, making it one of the most accessible urban fishing lakes in the southeastern United States. With 213 miles of shoreline across three counties and depths reaching 90 feet near the dam, this clear-water reservoir provides outstanding fishing just minutes from Nashville's downtown core, the Nashville International Airport, and the bustling suburbs of Smyrna and La Vergne. The lake's convenient location and excellent water quality make it the most heavily used recreational lake in the Nashville region.

Largemouth Bass are the most popular target at Percy Priest Lake, with anglers working the lake's rocky points, submerged creek channels, and scattered wood cover for quality fish throughout the year. Smallmouth Bass provide exciting fishing along the main lake's bluff walls and rocky banks, with the clear water allowing effective sight fishing during the spring spawn. Crappie fishing peaks during March and April when fish move into the back of creek arms and around brush piles. Catfish offer excellent summer and fall fishing in the deeper channel areas, while Striped Bass provide heart-pounding surface action when they corral shad schools in the open water.

Percy Priest Lake is exceptionally well served by public boat ramps, with Corps of Engineers facilities at Cook, Fate Sanders, Hamilton Creek, and numerous other locations around the lake. Long Hunter State Park on the lake's southeastern shore provides additional access along with hiking trails and picnic areas. Whether you are fishing a pre-work bass tournament at dawn, spending a family afternoon pulling Crappie near the dam, or chasing Striped Bass boils on a fall evening, Percy Priest Lake delivers quality fishing in the most convenient location imaginable for Nashville-area anglers.

name: Kentucky Lake (Tennessee)
description:
Kentucky Lake is the largest artificial lake east of the Mississippi River, and its Tennessee portion alone encompasses 160,300 acres across five counties with an astounding 2,064 miles of shoreline. Created by Kentucky Dam on the Tennessee River, this massive impoundment reaches depths of 75 feet and stretches from the Kentucky border south through the rolling hills of West Tennessee, offering a seemingly endless expanse of fishable water that supports one of the most productive warm-water fisheries in the nation. The towns of Paris, Camden, and New Johnsonville serve as primary access points on the Tennessee side of this legendary lake.

Crappie fishing is king on Kentucky Lake, with the Tennessee portion producing incredible catches of both black and white Crappie around the lake's extensive system of stakebeds, brush piles, and submerged timber. Spring Crappie fishing draws thousands of anglers to Paris Landing State Park and the Big Sandy area each year. Largemouth, Smallmouth, and Kentucky Bass provide outstanding fishing along the lake's ledges, points, and creek channel swings, with the famous ledge-fishing pattern producing impressive stringers during summer months. Catfish and Sauger round out the fishery, with channel and blue cats reaching impressive sizes in the main river channel.

Boat ramps are plentiful on the Tennessee portion of Kentucky Lake, with major facilities at Paris Landing State Park, Birdsong Marina near Camden, and New Johnsonville. The Tennessee National Wildlife Refuge encompasses significant portions of the lake's shoreline, providing habitat for both wildlife and fish. Whether you are pulling Crappie from brush piles in the Big Sandy embayment, cranking summer ledges for bass tournaments, or drifting the main channel for trophy Catfish, the Tennessee portion of Kentucky Lake delivers big-water fishing on a scale that is difficult to comprehend until you experience it firsthand.

name: Pickwick Lake (Tennessee)
description:
Pickwick Lake is a 43,100-acre TVA reservoir on the Tennessee River that straddles the borders of Tennessee, Alabama, and Mississippi, with the Tennessee portion spanning two counties and featuring 496 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 57 feet. This tri-state lake is nationally recognized as one of the finest Smallmouth Bass fisheries in the country, with the clear, current-swept waters of the Tennessee River channel providing ideal habitat for trophy-class bronzebacks. The town of Savannah and the community of Counce near Pickwick Dam serve as the primary staging areas for anglers fishing the Tennessee waters.

Smallmouth Bass are the headline species at Pickwick Lake, with the lake's rock-lined channel, shell beds, and current breaks producing outstanding numbers of fish in the 3 to 5 pound range along with legitimate trophy opportunities exceeding 6 pounds. The current from Wilson Dam upstream creates a river-like environment in portions of the lake that Smallmouth Bass thrive in. Largemouth Bass hold in the quieter creek arms and shallow coves, while Crappie provide excellent fishing around bridge pilings and submerged structure. Catfish, Sauger, and Striped Bass add diversity to the fishery, with the tailwater below Pickwick Dam being a particularly productive area for Sauger and Striped Bass.

Public boat ramps on the Tennessee side of Pickwick Lake include facilities at Pickwick Landing State Park, the community of Counce, and several TWRA-managed access points. Pickwick Landing State Park offers a full-service marina, lodge, and camping for visiting anglers. Whether you are dragging tubes across shell beds for trophy Smallmouth Bass, fishing the Pickwick Dam tailwater for Sauger and Stripers, or pulling Crappie from the bridge pilings along the main channel, Pickwick Lake delivers nationally ranked fishing in the scenic bluffs and bottomlands of Southwest Tennessee.

name: Reelfoot Lake
description:
Reelfoot Lake is one of the most unique natural lakes in North America, a 15,500-acre cypress swamp lake in the far northwest corner of Tennessee that was created by the catastrophic New Madrid earthquakes of 1811-1812. With 40 miles of shoreline across Lake and Obion counties and a maximum depth of just 18 feet, this hauntingly beautiful lake is filled with standing bald cypress and tupelo trees that rise from the dark, tannic water to create an otherworldly fishing landscape unlike anything else in the state. The small communities of Tiptonville and Samburg have served generations of anglers who make the pilgrimage to this legendary Crappie fishery.

Crappie fishing is the reason Reelfoot Lake is famous, with the lake consistently ranked among the top Crappie fisheries in the United States. The flooded cypress forest and abundant submerged structure provide perfect habitat for massive schools of black and white Crappie that reach impressive sizes on the lake's rich forage base. Bluegill and other sunfish offer outstanding panfish action in the shallow, warm waters around the cypress knees. Largemouth Bass fishing is excellent in the timber and lily pad fields, with the thick cover providing endless casting targets. Catfish and Bowfin, the prehistoric swamp survivors, round out a fishery that feels authentically wild.

Reelfoot Lake State Park and several privately operated resorts provide boat launch access and guide services for visiting anglers. Due to the shallow depth and extensive submerged timber, local knowledge is invaluable, and hiring a guide for a first visit is highly recommended. Whether you are threading your boat through the cypress cathedral to fill a stringer with Crappie, casting buzzbaits over lily pads for Largemouth Bass, or marveling at the bald eagles and great blue herons that share this remarkable ecosystem, Reelfoot Lake delivers a fishing experience that is as much about the place itself as the fish you catch.

name: Tims Ford Lake
description:
Tims Ford Lake is a 10,700-acre Tennessee Valley Authority reservoir on the Elk River in the scenic Highland Rim of southern Middle Tennessee, spanning three counties with 265 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 90 feet. This deep, clear-water lake is surrounded by rolling hills, hardwood forests, and the charming small towns of Winchester, Tullahoma, and Lynchburg, the latter being the world-famous home of Jack Daniel's Tennessee Whiskey distillery. The lake's excellent water quality and diverse structure support a well-balanced fishery that draws anglers from across the region.

Smallmouth Bass are the premier species at Tims Ford Lake, with the lake's clear water, rocky bluffs, and chunk rock banks creating ideal habitat for bronzebacks that routinely reach 3 to 4 pounds. Largemouth Bass provide excellent fishing in the creek arms and around dock structures, with the lake producing quality fish throughout the season. Walleye are a prized target during winter and early spring, with anglers targeting the deeper main-lake structure and Elk River channel. Crappie fishing peaks during the spring spawn around brush piles and stakebeds, while Catfish and Striped Bass add big-fish potential to the diverse fishery.

Tims Ford State Park provides the primary boat launch facility and offers camping, cabins, a marina, and a championship golf course overlooking the lake. Additional public ramps are available at Holiday Marina and several county-operated access points. Whether you are pitching jigs to rocky bluffs for Smallmouth Bass, trolling the main channel for Walleye, or combining a fishing trip with a visit to the Jack Daniel's distillery in nearby Lynchburg, Tims Ford Lake offers an exceptional blend of quality fishing and southern Tennessee charm.

name: Tennessee River
description:
The Tennessee River is the main artery of Tennessee's waterway system, flowing 652 miles of shoreline through nine counties as it crosses the entire width of the state from the Appalachian foothills to the Mississippi River floodplain. With depths reaching 60 feet in the main navigation channel, this mighty river and its series of TVA and Corps of Engineers impoundments create one of the most extensive and productive freshwater fishing systems in the southeastern United States. From the urban waterfront of Nashville on the Cumberland River tributary to the rural bluffs near Savannah, the Tennessee River system offers an extraordinary diversity of fishing environments and species.

Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass fishing on the Tennessee River is nationally recognized, with the river's extensive system of dams creating tailwater and reservoir habitats that support thriving populations of both species. Catfish of all three major species, including trophy Blue Catfish exceeding 50 pounds, inhabit the deeper channel areas and tailwaters throughout the river system. Sauger and Striped Bass concentrate below the dams during cooler months, providing outstanding action for anglers targeting these current-loving predators. Crappie and Drum round out the diverse fishery, with Crappie fishing being a beloved tradition across the river's many embayments and creek arms.

Boat ramps are abundant along the entire Tennessee River corridor, with TVA, Corps of Engineers, state, and county facilities providing access at regular intervals. Major launch points include facilities near Nashville, Savannah, Clifton, and Waverly. Whether you are fishing the tailwater below a TVA dam for Sauger and Striped Bass, drifting the main channel for heavyweight Blue Catfish, or exploring the quieter backwater sloughs for Largemouth Bass and Crappie, the Tennessee River delivers the full breadth of Southern freshwater fishing across the entire width of the Volunteer State.

name: Great Smoky Mountains Streams
description:
The Great Smoky Mountains National Park contains over 2,100 miles of fishable streams across three Tennessee counties, making it one of the largest and most diverse trout fishing destinations in the eastern United States. With depths rarely exceeding 10 feet even in the largest pools, these mountain streams cascade through ancient forests of hemlock, rhododendron, and hardwood, tumbling over mossy boulders and sliding through crystal-clear pools that support wild populations of Brook Trout, Rainbow Trout, and Brown Trout. The gateway communities of Gatlinburg, Townsend, and Cosby provide lodging, supplies, and guide services for the hundreds of thousands of anglers who wet a line in the park each year.

Brook Trout are the crown jewel of the Smokies fishery, with the park protecting the southernmost native populations of this colorful char in streams above 3,000 feet elevation. Rainbow Trout are the most abundant species in the mid-elevation streams, with wild fish averaging 6 to 10 inches providing eager rises to dry flies and nymphs. Brown Trout inhabit the lower-elevation streams and larger rivers within the park, growing to respectable sizes where deeper pools and undercut banks provide cover. Smallmouth Bass are found in the lower reaches of the larger streams as they exit the park, adding warm-water variety to the predominantly cold-water fishery.

Access to Smokies streams is available from numerous trailheads and roadside pulloffs along Little River Road near Townsend, Newfound Gap Road through Gatlinburg, and the remote Cataloochee Valley. The park requires no fishing license for its Tennessee waters but does require anglers to follow strict single-hook artificial-lure regulations designed to protect wild trout populations. Whether you are hiking to a remote headwater stream for native Brook Trout, casting dry flies to wild Rainbows in the shadows of Clingmans Dome, or wading the Little River near Townsend for Brown Trout, the Great Smoky Mountains streams offer a backcountry trout fishing experience that is sacred ground for Appalachian anglers.

name: Watauga Lake
description:
Watauga Lake is a 6,430-acre TVA reservoir nestled in the Appalachian Mountains of upper East Tennessee, with 104 miles of shoreline in Carter and Johnson counties and an impressive maximum depth of 285 feet that makes it one of the deepest lakes in the state. This stunning mountain lake sits at 1,959 feet elevation and is surrounded by the Cherokee National Forest, with steep, forested ridges plunging directly into cold, clear water that maintains excellent quality throughout the year. The town of Elizabethton serves as the gateway to the lake, while the community of Butler, which was relocated when the lake was filled, and Hampton provide additional access.

Smallmouth Bass are the premier gamefish at Watauga Lake, with the deep, clear water and rocky shoreline providing outstanding habitat for bronzebacks that regularly exceed 3 pounds. Walleye thrive in the lake's deep, cold waters and provide excellent fishing during the cooler months when they move to shallower structure. Rainbow and Brown Trout inhabit the deeper portions of the lake and are particularly abundant in the cold-water discharge below Watauga Dam, where a world-class tailwater trout fishery has developed. Largemouth Bass and Crappie provide additional fishing opportunities in the sheltered coves and creek arms.

Boat launches are available at Watauga Point and several other locations around the lake, with the facilities at Watauga Point being the most popular and well-maintained. The lake's deep, narrow character and mountain setting make it a beautiful destination for boating and fishing, though the steep terrain limits bank access in many areas. Whether you are working a tube jig along a rocky bluff for Smallmouth Bass, trolling deep for Walleye in the clear mountain water, or fishing the legendary tailwater below the dam for trophy Brown Trout, Watauga Lake offers a premier Appalachian mountain fishing experience in the highlands of East Tennessee.

name: Boone Lake
description:
Boone Lake is a 4,400-acre TVA reservoir at the confluence of the South Fork Holston River and Watauga River in the Tri-Cities region of upper East Tennessee. With 130 miles of shoreline across Sullivan and Washington counties and depths reaching 100 feet, this scenic mountain lake serves as the home fishery for the combined metropolitan area of Johnson City, Kingsport, and Bristol, providing convenient access to quality fishing for over 500,000 residents. The lake winds through the rolling Appalachian foothills and offers a pleasant mix of residential shoreline and undeveloped natural areas.

Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass provide the backbone of the Boone Lake fishery, with both species benefiting from the lake's diverse habitat of docks, rocky points, and creek channel structure. Crappie fishing is outstanding during the spring spawn when fish push into the shallow coves and around brush piles and dock pilings. Walleye have been stocked and provide a growing fishery that excites cool-weather anglers targeting the deeper main-lake structure. Catfish offer excellent fishing in the river channel areas, and Striped Bass add big-fish excitement with their open-water surface-feeding frenzies during the fall shad migration.

Boone Lake offers several public boat ramps, including facilities at Boone Dam and multiple TVA-managed access points spread across the two-county shoreline. The lake recently underwent a multi-year TVA dam repair project that included shoreline improvements. Whether you are skipping jigs under docks for Largemouth Bass, jigging for Walleye in the deep river channel, or chasing Striped Bass boils on an autumn evening, Boone Lake delivers reliable fishing in the scenic Appalachian foothills of the Tri-Cities region.

name: South Holston Lake
description:
South Holston Lake is a 7,580-acre TVA reservoir straddling the Tennessee-Virginia border in Sullivan County, with 168 miles of shoreline and depths plunging to 230 feet in this deep Appalachian mountain impoundment. The lake is surrounded by the Cherokee and Jefferson National Forests, with steep, forested ridges creating a remote mountain atmosphere despite the lake's proximity to Bristol and the Tri-Cities metropolitan area. South Holston is perhaps best known for the world-class tailwater trout fishery below its dam, where the South Holston River has earned a reputation as one of the premier dry-fly streams in the eastern United States.

Smallmouth Bass are the top lake species at South Holston, with the deep, clear water and rocky structure producing quality bronzebacks that thrive in the lake's cold, well-oxygenated depths. Walleye provide excellent fishing in the main lake during winter and spring, with fish often exceeding 5 pounds taken from the deeper channel areas. Largemouth Bass and Crappie inhabit the creek arms and sheltered coves, providing reliable fishing during warmer months. The tailwater below South Holston Dam is the true gem, with Rainbow and Brown Trout feeding on prolific sulphur hatches that create some of the most technical and rewarding dry-fly fishing anywhere in the South.

Boat launches are available at several locations around South Holston Lake, with TVA-managed ramps at Observation Knob and other access points providing well-maintained facilities. The tailwater section is accessible from numerous wading access points along the South Holston River below the dam. Whether you are drop-shotting rocky ledges for Smallmouth Bass on the main lake, trolling for Walleye in the deep mountain water, or matching a sulphur hatch on the world-famous tailwater below the dam, South Holston Lake and its tailwater deliver an extraordinary combination of lake and river fishing in the Appalachian highlands of upper East Tennessee.
